summary: Stancil argues that LinkedIn has become a peculiar, self-contained social network where professional performance replaces genuine social life, creating an artificial feedback loop of engagement and influence. He likens it to a Truman Show-style simulation: a place that feels socially rewarding but is insulated from real-world culture, celebrity, and spontaneity.
money_quotes: It’s not socializing; it’s editorial calendar crosstalk.
It’s a vault, locked on both sides.

LinkedIn offers us a deal: Don’t really be yourself; don’t talk about taboo things; put up a professional headshot; feign excitement about your new role managing enterprise IoT applications on the edge.
The conversation is real, even if the motivations are more suspect.
Offer up your personality and a few self-promotional posts, and LinkedIn will make you feel like a celebrity.
key_insight: LinkedIn succeeds by rewarding sanitized, professional self-performance with the feelings of popularity and connection, even as it remains cut off from real cultural life.
